[153:08]bombing their islands, bombing their southern coast,

[153:11]bombing IRGC vessels in the Persian Gulf.

[153:15]And as the United States is carrying out this attack,

[153:18]now Iran is retaliating against the United States,

[153:21]bombing our bases in Kuwait and Bahrain.

[153:25]The United States has also removed the sanctions waivers, revoked them, and so now Iranian

[153:32]oil will once again be subject to the U.S. sanctions regime and will not be allowed to

[153:37]be officially sold on the open market.

[153:40]And this is a story from the Wall Street Journal about the events of the past 24 hours.

[153:46]And we'll talk a little bit more about it.

[153:49]The Trump administration pounded sites along Iran's coast and fresh airstrikes and blocked

[153:54]its ability to sell oil legally on Tuesday in response to Tehran's recent attacks on

[154:00]ships near the Strait of Hormuz.

[154:02]The strikes on Tuesday were four to five times more extensive than other attacks launched

[154:06]since the agreement to end the war, which was signed last month.

[154:13]The strikes targeted air defense, coastal surveillance, surface to air and anti-ship cruise

[154:18]missile sites as well as drone launch sites and port facilities. The attacks came shortly after

[154:24]the Trump administration revoked a license allowing Iran to sell oil on the open market.

[154:29]The Treasury Department said the June 21st temporary license granted to Iran after several

[154:34]months of war would no longer apply. But the Treasury Department allowed for a grace period

[154:41]for transactions already authorized under the license.

[154:45]Iran's foreign ministry criticized the move and said in a statement that the revocation of the

[154:49]waivers was a violation of the memorandum of understanding. The U.S. has continued to coordinate

[154:55]with commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz using a route that it cleared near the

[155:00]coast of Oman. Over the weekend, the IRGC warned ships that it was prepared to target them if they

[155:08]Early Tuesday, Iran fired anti-ship cruise missiles

[155:11]and one-way attack drones at vessels seeking to cross

[155:15]the southern route, three ships were struck.

[155:18]Iran's attacks come just as traffic

[155:20]through the Strait of Hormuz as recovering.

[155:22]Daily traffic through the checkpoint is stabilized

[155:24]at between 30 to 60 crossings in recent days.

[155:28]So let's get into the background a little bit.

[155:33]If we are to reframe the conflict in Iran,

[155:40]that the nature of the U.S. war with Iran

[155:43]has fundamentally changed.

[155:46]And it changed the moment that we killed the Ayatollah.

[155:51]And the way that it has changed

[155:52]is in terms of the fundamental American strategic objective.

[155:58]We started hostilities with Iran last year in June,

[156:03]when we bombed Iran's nuclear sites

[156:05]in Operation Midnight Hammer.

[156:07]And so you could say that the initial goals of U.S. hostilities against Iran were primarily

[156:14]about shutting down Iran's enrichment capability and destroying Iran's nuclear stockpile.

[156:21]When we went to war again this year, on February 27th, we killed the Ayatollah, we bombed

[156:34]That's why we decapitated the regime.

[156:37]We killed the supreme leader, decimated the military and civilian leadership, and we

[156:42]attacked not only political regime targets, but also military targets across the country.

[156:49]But look beyond this.

[156:50]What was the purpose of the regime change?

[156:53]Fundamentally, the reason we sought regime change is because Iran would not agree to our

[157:01]ultimatum to give up their nuclear centrifuges.

[157:05]So realistically, the regime change was also about Iran's nuclear program.

[157:11]Last year, we sought to arrest the development of their nuclear program by bombing it, making

[157:21]Because we could not convince them through negotiations

[157:24]to abandon their ambitions for a nuclear weapon

[157:28]or a nuclear threshold state, we sought a regime change.

[157:32]Because if they had a different regime,

[157:34]then that new regime would not be pursuing

[157:37]a nuclear arsenal.

[157:39]But the second that we attacked the regime

[157:43]and we sought its ouster,

[157:44]hoping that the people of Iran would rise up,

[157:47]failed in doing this suddenly the war became about something else because the

[157:54]moment that we failed at pushing the regime out Iran closed the Strait of

[157:59]Hormuz

[158:00]This is something that the United States civilian leadership did not anticipate the military leadership

[158:06]The Pentagon knew they were going to close the straight and they were warning us in the press

[158:10]If you go back and read the press in February of this year in the weeks leading up to the conflict

[158:16]There were planted stories in Politico in the Washington Post coming from the Pentagon that said regime change will fail

[158:23]They'll close the straight

[158:25]And what we've learned since is that it was the president

[158:28]and some of his advisors who were not confident

[158:32]that Iran would be willing or able to do this

[158:35]or sustain the closure of the strait.

[158:38]So we failed that regime change

[158:41]and what Iran immediately did is close the strait.

[158:44]Now, what this means is that everything since then

[158:48]has fundamentally now become about

[158:52]opening up the Strait of Hormuz.

[158:55]We're going after their nukes last year.

[158:58]This year we're going after regime change to get them to abandon their nuclear ambitions

[159:03]or aspirations.

[159:05]But then because we went for the regime, they closed the straight.

[159:09]Everything since then has now been about opening up the Strait of Hormuz.

[159:14]We now recognize and realize we're not gonna topple the regime.

[159:19]The regime is entrenched, it's dug in, they're not gonna negotiate.

[159:24]We recognize now we really can't militarily defeat them without some kind of ground force

[159:30]The air strikes the naval strikes. It's not enough

[159:34]We sank their navy that really doesn't matter from a strategic point of view

[159:38]We've bombed them and thrown everything at them has not meaningfully suppressed or destroyed their missile and drone capability

[159:45]So what is the primary objective now? Well now since the Strait of Hormuz has been closed

[159:51]economically, we're bleeding

[159:53]In order to keep energy prices stable,

[159:56]we've had to open up our strategic petroleum reserve,

[159:59]as everybody knows, and not only that,

[160:01]but we've coordinated with the International Energy Agency

[160:04]to open up all these strategic reserves

[160:06]in all the different countries in the world.

[160:08]Hundreds of millions of barrels of oil

[160:10]have been opened up in other oil reserves in other countries.

[160:15]And this has only just bought us time,

[160:18]but we recognize that when those reserves run out,

[160:21]when those last shipments from the straight arrive at their ports, which they already have at their

[160:26]destination, then the clock is going to start ticking for a total economic meltdown for when

[160:33]there's legitimate energy scarcity. And when I say energy scarcity, I don't mean gas prices are high,

[160:38]I mean, there are fuel shortages. Because at a certain point, it just becomes a question of

[160:48]into the pipelines to go to consumers.

[160:51]How do we get enough of the right kind of oil

[160:53]to our refining infrastructure and get it out?

[160:56]You're just going to face real shortages

[160:59]where people are not gonna be able

[161:00]to get the energy they need.

[161:02]And what happens then is people panic.

[161:05]And then people are going to start

[161:07]to try to stockpile energy.

[161:09]You see this happening in Russia.

[161:10]This is sort of a different situation.

[161:12]Ukraine is increasing their attacks on Russia's

[161:15]oil refineries.

[161:17]And so there are legitimate fuel shortages for cars.

[161:20]So what happens when gas prices start to shoot up

[161:23]is people start to hoard the gas.

[161:26]And that means there's an even bigger pressure on supply

[161:29]and then you have a real crisis.

[161:32]Now you have the state having to intervene,

[161:33]now it's a real problem.

[161:35]And so this has been the fundamental issue ever since basically February 28th is how

[161:40]do we now get the Strait of Hormuz open and end the war?

[161:44]Okay, we're not getting regime change.

[161:46]We're not going to destroy their nukes or their stockpile of uranium.

[161:50]We're not going to suppress their drones and missiles.

Topic 7: Part 2 (opening the Strait & why Trump can't end the war)

[161:52]How do we now just get them to agree to open up the strait so we can wrap it up?

[161:58]Iran has us by the balls.

[162:00]And they know it.

[162:01]And everything that has taken place since February 28th has been an effort, militarily

[162:06]or through negotiations, to try to get Iran to let us leave the conflict.

[162:13]A lot of people think it's up to us to just walk away.

[162:16]People say, why doesn't Trump just end the war?

[162:18]I'm sure he wants to.

[162:20]I'm sure he knows he needs to, but he can't.

[162:28]We can't let them do that and so it's a question of whether Iran will let us

[162:34]Iran could end the conflict tomorrow if they simply open up the straight to traffic

[162:39]With freedom of navigation, which was a status quo before the war

[162:44]But they won't do that. They won't let us leave

[162:47]because they want to take control over the straight.

[162:51]And the reason being is because that capability

[162:55]will just be another tool in their arsenal

[162:57]to deter us from attacking them again.

[163:00]In the past, the United States was wary about attacking Iran because we were worried that

[163:06]Hezbollah would attack Israel.

[163:08]Iran would use its ballistic missiles to target Israel and American bases and our Arab allies.

[163:13]They might create a weapon.

[163:16]They might weaponize their nuclear program.

[163:18]And now what Iran wants to do is add another form of deterrence, which is we're going to

[163:26]We are going to more easily and officially, and maybe even legally, shut down the Strait

[163:33]of Hormuz.

[163:34]And that way, the next time the United States goes to war with Iran in a coalition with

[163:38]Israel and the Gulf States, not only is Iran going to weaponize their nukes, not only is

[163:44]Hezbollah going to bomb Israel, not only is Iran going to bomb everybody with missiles,

[163:48]but also they're going to shut down the Strait and there's nothing we could do about it.

[163:56]And Iran has basically said this openly.

[163:59]They said this explicitly a couple of weeks ago.

[164:02]We covered it on the show.

[164:03]Iran's leader said, we're not interested in normalization so much.

[164:08]They said, we're actually not that interested in bilateral relations with the United States,

[164:15]integrating into the region.

[164:16]We're not necessarily interested in some formal peace treaty where we're on good terms with

[164:22]the people that tried to destroy us.

[164:24]They said, we're interested in ensuring that they never attack us again.

[164:28]He said, we're more interested in ensuring a durable peace, meaning that even if the

[164:33]United States doesn't like us, even if Israel doesn't like us, even if there's no formal

[164:37]peace treaty that ends the war, they're not going to try it again because of deterrence,

[164:43]because Iran has reestablished deterrence, which in a word is an enforcement mechanism.

[164:49]Iran is basically saying, we don't trust your treaties.

[164:53]We're not interested in a handshake, a photo op.

[164:56]We're not interested in some treaty like the JCPOA, which the United States ripped up.

[165:01]They said that doesn't guarantee our security.

[165:05]All the pomp and circumstance, it's nice, but we saw what happened the last time.

[165:10]Iran made a deal with the United States and the European three countries and Russia and

[165:14]China.

[165:15]It was called the JCPOA, a Zionist puppet, Trump got elected and ripped it up and started

[165:21]attacking Iran, engaging in a shadow campaign against them with Israel, and then outright

[165:26]attacked Iran many times.

[165:29]So Iran says, you can keep all that.

[165:30]We're not interested in that.

[165:32]What we are now principally concerned with is making sure that you never want to attack

[165:37]us again.

[165:38]They're changing the incentives.

[165:40]Now, the United States cannot leave until the Strait of Hormuz is open for a few reasons.

[165:46]One, because we happen to know this.

[165:50]We know that if Iran controls the Strait, that that will make it impossible to ever

[165:55]go in and manage this problem again.

[165:58]And by this problem, I mean Iran still has nuclear enrichment.

[166:03]They still have their stockpile of highly enriched uranium.

[166:07]They still have a massive native drone and missile production capability.

[166:12]And so if Iran gains control over the straight and they take military intervention off the

[166:17]table forever through deterrence, then that means that we basically have no way to intervene

[166:27]Let's say we make a nuclear agreement with Iran.

[166:31]If Iran breaks the terms of the agreement, how do we punish them?

[166:35]If they control the strait, there's no way for us to go in, can't bomb them, can't pursue

[166:41]regime change, can't go to war even in a limited or narrow way, because then they'll choke

[166:47]off the strait and destroy the global economy again.

[166:51]It'll alienate us from NATO, from our partners in the Pacific, so we know this.

[166:57]If Iran controls the strait, they're going to make money from it.

[167:01]We now have effectively seated control of one of the world's most vital waterways with

[167:05]20% of the world's oil, and then they get to charge money off of this.

[167:10]So this is a vital strategic waterway that is under the control of one of our defiant

[167:15]mortal enemies.

[167:17]And then they're going to profit from it and use the proceeds to fund the activities that

[167:21]we're concerned about, which is rebuilding their drone and missile infrastructure, advancing

[167:26]their nuclear program, funding their proxies like Hezbollah, the Houthis, the militias

[167:32]inside of Iraq.

[167:35]And then maybe the tertiary concern

[167:37]is that if Iran gains control over a vital waterway,

[167:41]annexes it, uses it to profit,

[167:45]we're also concerned about the precedent

[167:47]this establishes for other vital waterways.

[167:50]Are other countries going to look?

[167:53]some of these choke points for global trade and are they going to start to

[167:57]want to charge money? Maybe Turkey is going to start to charge for the

[168:01]Bosporus trade or maybe Malaysia or Singapore, Indonesia will claim control

[168:06]over some of the passages into the South China Sea from the Indian Ocean. It's

[168:12]another problem about setting that precedent in general. The United States

[168:16]is the guarantor of global security and the rules-based order. We have to ensure

[168:23]So these are basically our concerns from a strategic point of view.

[168:27]We cannot allow Iran to come out the victor.

[168:32]That's a huge spoils.

[168:33]It's a huge prize for Iran to walk away from our all out attack with control over this

[168:38]vital waterway and then basically all the leverage in any future diplomacy or relationship

[168:46]with the United States and our partners in the Middle East.

[168:50]So, you understand basically now that this is the strategic issue at the heart of what

[168:56]is happening with U.S. and Iran.

[168:59]When we're bombing Iran in any form and any capacity since the original ceasefire, we're

[169:04]trying to compel them through the threat of force or assassinations to open up the strait

[169:10]and let us leave.

[169:11]The negotiations are accomplishing the exact same thing.

[169:15]That has been the central point of contention,

[169:17]even since the original ceasefire,

[169:19]is we are telling Iran, look, we'll stop bombing you,

[169:23]but for us to leave you alone,

[169:25]we need you to open up the straight

[169:26]so that we could get our oil back,

[169:28]we could get our economy going,

[169:29]but the terms under which the straight must open

[169:32]is freedom of navigation.

[169:34]And Iran has not been willing to do this.

[169:36]And so that's why even since the original ceasefire,

[169:39]we had to negotiate the MOU.

[169:42]But inside the memorandum of understanding, which is the latest agreement, which we talked

Topic 8: Part 3 (the MOU & the Strait's vague terms)

[169:46]about as it was announced, and they formally agreed to it in June, is that the actual provisions

[169:55]inside the MOU are totally ambiguous about the straight.

[169:59]What the memorandum of understanding says is that the Strait of Hormuz will open

[170:04]Normal traffic must resume within 30 days of the signing of the agreement and normal traffic is about 130 to

[170:13]160 ships per day something like that

[170:15]That was the pre-war volume of shipping

[170:18]But it also says that the Strait will be jointly managed by Iran and Oman

[170:23]And so what exactly does that mean? Well, nobody knows because it doesn't say

[170:28]The provision says the strait will open, normal traffic must resume within 30 days, and it

[170:34]will be jointly managed by Iran and Oman, which border the strait with consultation

[170:40]from the other Gulf countries, which are Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, the UAE.

[170:47]And so Iran has met with Oman and they've discussed this and there's some disagreement

[170:51]about what will happen there.

[170:52]This is what brings us to what has happened today.

[170:55]So since the MOU, there's been this uneasy understanding about the strait.

[171:01]Iran has said that they're implementing the agreement by gradually opening up the strait

[171:07]of Hormuz and allowing traffic to resume.

[171:10]What Iran says is that this is going to be a managed opening.

[171:15]It's going to be a controlled opening.

[171:18]And they're saying that in order to ensure safe passage in the strait, all of the commercial

[171:23]ships that transit have to get permission from the IRGC first and only use approved

[171:30]routes.

[171:31]They say they're conducting de-mining operations.

[171:37]And they're also effectively enforcing

[171:39]something like a blockade.

[171:41]They're threatening to shoot on any ship

[171:43]that doesn't have permission.

[171:44]And so Iran is saying, look, the Strait is opening.

[171:47]We're allowing traffic to go through,

[171:50]but we're gonna need you to tell us before you're coming

[171:53]and you can only go through these routes.

[171:55]Otherwise we'll attack you.

[171:58]So this is how it worked as the MOU was passed.

[172:02]These ships were coordinating with the IRGC.

[172:04]They were being allowed to leave the Strait.

[172:07]And traffic was picking back up to about 30, 35 ships

[172:11]on average per day.

[172:13]But then what started to happen about two weeks ago

[172:16]is that a second route was opened up

[172:18]on the southern side of the strait.

[172:20]So the strait of Hormuz is bounded on the north by Iran

[172:24]and Iran's islands, and on the south by Oman,

[172:27]which is a far weaker country with a much smaller population

[172:30]and which is relatively neutral

[172:32]between Iran and the United States.

[172:34]So the United States in conjunction with Oman has opened up this second route on the southern

[172:39]side, which ships have been using without getting permission from Iran.

[172:46]And so you could say these two different routes represent the two different strategic ideas

[172:50]about what will happen in the straight.

[172:52]Iran wants to establish the precedent that to use the straight, you need permission from

[172:56]them first.

[172:57]Why is that?

[172:58]Because, if ships start to get used to the idea that they need Iran's permission to go

[173:04]through the strait, then outside the life of this agreement, which expires in 60 days,

[173:10]then Iran will only grant permission if you pay Iran.

[173:15]That's how Iran wants this to evolve.

[173:17]They want everybody to get used to this precedent, that if you want to use the strait, you need

[173:21]Iran's permission.

[173:23]Why would you need permission?

[173:24]Well, because they own it.

[173:26]And if they own it, they can seek a rent off of it as well.

[173:30]And so in the future, they're going to say,

[173:32]well, we're only giving permission to go through the straight

[173:34]if you pay our $1 per barrel of oil fee

[173:38]or $2 million per ship fee.

[173:41]And they're going to finesse the language.

[173:42]They're saying, well, this is a fee for services rendered,

[173:46]environmental fees, protection fees, service fees.

[173:49]This is our payoff to manage the straight.

[173:53]And so Iran wants every ship to go through their approved route and they're going to enforce this because that will establish the precedent for their legal formal claim over the straight, which will give them the right in the future to charge money to use it.

[174:08]Now, if Oman opens up a free lane, if they open up a route that can be used for free without permission, then obviously this is a threat to Iran's claim, their monopolistic claim of controlling the straight.

[174:23]And what started to happen in the past two weeks is that the volume of shipping going

[174:27]through the strait went up dramatically because all the ships started to go through the free

[174:32]route, hugging the coast of Oman.

[174:36]And so what happened, not last Friday, but the Friday before that, is that Iran began

[174:41]attacking those ships with drones and missiles.

[174:44]And Iran's supreme leader and their jurist counsel said that for this route to exist,

[174:50]threatens Iran's control over the straight, which is all their leverage, which is effectively

[174:54]all their deterrent power.

[174:56]They said it can't be allowed to go on.

[174:58]So they started to attack this shipping every single day, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday,

[175:04]not last week, but the week before that, that final week in June.

[175:08]In response, the United States bombed Iran's southern coast from where they were launching

[175:13]these attacks, bombing drone launch sites, missile launch sites on Iran's islands and

[175:18]on their southern coast as a direct and proportional retaliation for those attacks.

[175:24]On top of that, the United States began escorting all commercial shipping with a full package.

[175:31]Drones and Ospreys and fighter jets, individual ships were getting a full package to go through

[175:37]to deter Iran from targeting them with drones.

[175:41]Over the past week, things have been relatively quiet.

[175:44]Iran has been hosting the funeral

[175:46]of their Supreme Leader Ali Hamanay.

[175:49]And so they've had delegations from every country,

[175:51]I think over a hundred countries came to visit,

[175:53]including Saudi Arabia, including Qatar,

[175:56]including all the countries involved in the conflict.

[176:00]But then this started up all over again last night.

Topic 9: Part 4 (back and forth bombings & failed objectives)

[176:03]Ships have continued to use that southern route hugging the coast of Oman.

[176:08]And once again, last night, Iran targeted three ships shutting this down.

[176:14]Now the big development today is that the United States has launched an all out attack

[176:17]on Iran.

[176:18]But like I said, this one is different.

[176:20]Last week the strike was proportional so Iran was launching one-way attack drones at the ships

[176:27]We were shooting down the drones and attacking the bases from which the drones came

[176:31]Very proportional, and this was put to put Iran in check

[176:36]Saying if you attack those ships, we're gonna shoot down the projectiles. We're gonna bomb the sites where they came from

[176:43]Well, now the US response was not proportional and the US central command under the Pentagon

[176:50]basically said this to the press.

[176:52]They said the goal of this strike is not to be proportional.

[176:55]This is to send a message to the leadership in Tehran, which is if you keep messing around

[177:01]and you don't leave our shipping alone, we're going back to war.

[177:05]So, we launched a strike against about 80 different targets, 60 Iranian ships, and about

[177:11]20 sites on Iran's coast, and on its islands, it was a powerful strike, intended to send

[177:18]the message that, like I said, you have to enforce your part of the MOU and let the

[177:23]shipping go through.

[177:25]In response, Iran is now launching a major drone and missile attack against US bases

[177:33]And now, I assume the United States will retaliate for this.

[177:37]And so it looks like the MOU has basically fallen apart.

[177:40]Like I said, the other big part of this is not only did the U.S. bomb Iran disproportionately,

[177:46]but today we also announced that we're revoking the sanctions waiver given to them in the

[177:51]MOU.

[177:52]In order to even get Iran to agree to this 60-day memorandum of understanding, which

[178:02]We haven't even started implementing it.

[178:05]But part of the sweetener to get Iran even in the agreement in the first place was up front,

[178:10]we ended our blockade of their shipping.

[178:13]So ever since the MOU started, Iran has been freely importing and exporting through the Strait.

[178:19]We have left their ports alone.

[178:21]Meanwhile, the Strait is still closed for us.

[178:24]And secondarily, the other thing they got up front is that we gave a waiver on US sanctions against their oil.

[178:31]So, ever since the MOU went into effect, Iran has been able to sell its oil at full price

[178:38]on the open market, and they've been able to denominate this trade in dollars, which

[178:44]means that they have got an immediate financial relief.

[178:49]In the previous regime, we were not allowing them to use US dollars.

[178:54]We banned them from the US dollar system and so not only did they have to sell their oil

[178:58]on the black market with their shadow fleet to select customers at a discount, but also

[179:03]was very arduous to receive the payment for the oil.

[179:07]It took a longer period of time because they had to go through other currencies.

[179:11]Well, not only did we let them sell the oil on the open market, which means they can charge

[179:16]full price.

[179:17]They can sell to a variety of customers, so it's competitive.

[179:20]But also we let them settle the payments in dollars.

[179:23]They're getting billions and billions of dollars from the sale of their oil settled immediately.

[179:28]They're getting that cash upfront.

[179:31]So, what it's looking like, and this is what I said when the MOU happened, it's two things.

[179:36]What I said about the MOU when it was signed is that, one, it's a giveaway for Iran.

[179:41]They're getting things up front without giving anything at all.

[179:46]And what they got up front is that we ended our blockade of air shipping.

[179:49]Meanwhile, the straight remained closed, and they slow walked it.

[179:53]They said, well, we can't open up the straight until we get rid of all the mines.

[180:01]So, they slow walked it, meanwhile we left them alone.

[180:05]They got the sanctions waiver on the oil, so they sold tons of oil, made billions of

[180:09]dollars, they got financial relief up front.

[180:12]The third thing they got is they get to keep their nuclear program going under the previous

[180:17]status quo.

[180:18]So, even though the first war was about limiting and restraining their nuclear program,

[180:24]And the second war was about regime change

[180:26]to get them to abandon the development

[180:29]of some kind of threshold status

[180:31]with their nuclear program.

[180:32]Under this agreement, there's no restrictions on it.

[180:36]As we negotiate restrictions on the nuclear program,

[180:39]they're moving full steam ahead.

[180:41]And the status quo prevails.

[180:42]That's the language of the MOU.

[180:45]So they get all of this upfront.

[180:47]No expectations on the nuclear program.

[180:49]They get to sell their oil in dollars,

[180:53]immediately. And we immediately stop the blockade of their ports. So it's a total giveaway.

[180:59]We get nothing. We get these vague promises that in the future, they might consider inspections

[181:05]on Natanz, Esfahan, and Fordo. They might consider some voluntary restrictions on nuclear enrichment,

[181:13]but not giving it up. They might consider downblending or diluting their nuclear stockpile under the

[181:23]But they didn't do any of those things.

[181:24]They made vague promises to talk about doing those things in the future once they got everything

[181:30]they asked for upfront.

[181:32]There was even discussion last week that the United States would give Iran $6 billion of

[181:38]their unfrozen assets to keep them in the deal.

[181:42]So we give them all this stuff upfront to get them to agree to it.

[181:47]They don't open up the straight.

[181:50]We know what they're doing, they're finessing and manipulating us, and we're going to give

[181:54]them billions dollars more to keep them in the process, which didn't actually happen,

[182:01]as so we're told, but that's something that was offered to them.

[182:04]So that's number one about the MOU.

[182:07]To this day, three, four weeks in, we realized this was a giveaway.

[182:17]They have not opened up the straight this weekend 30 to 60 ships per day

[182:22]Transited the straight, but on average it was about 37

[182:27]It was roughly 37 ships on average per day

[182:31]Which is only a little bit higher than the highest it's been since the war started in February

[182:38]Which was back when the ceasefire happened in April. I think it was the high of 30 ships went through the straight on a given day

[182:45]So, the traffic is not normalized, we have gotten nothing so far.

[182:51]But the second big part of the MOU is that it didn't actually fundamentally resolve anything.

[182:57]If in order to end this conflict, we needed to resolve three things, which is one, how

[183:03]we're going to end hostilities with Iran, two, how we're going to open up the strait

[183:08]and three, what the future negotiations will look like over the nuclear program.

[183:13]The MOU didn't solve any of these, because like I said from the beginning, there was

[183:17]no explicit agreement about what would be done about Lebanon.

[183:22]The MOU said it's gonna be a ceasefire on every front or no deal and that includes Lebanon

[183:29]Well, we're not the ones bombing Lebanon. Israel is and Israel wasn't even a party to the agreement

[183:35]Israel wasn't present Israel never

[183:38]Affirmatively agreed to it as a matter of fact they explicitly said we're gonna do the opposite of this

[183:44]We're not bound or constrained by it and there's no good will we're not gonna go with the United States and implement this

[183:51]So from the get-go, literally provision number one of the MOU was never going to happen because

[183:57]provision number one said Lebanon's territorial integrity and sovereignty must be respected.

[184:04]This is something that Israel must enforce.

[184:07]It's something Israel never agreed to, wasn't a party to, wasn't consulted on, and they're

[184:12]never going to agree to that.

[184:17]Number two is the straight like we talked about since February 27th

[184:21]The question has been under what conditions and how and when will Iran open up the straight?

[184:27]Iran has insisted that the only way the straight opens is under their legal control and we have said

[184:34]That's never gonna happen with the only way we will accept an opening of the straight and end hostilities as if it's freedom of navigation

[184:42]And the day the MOU was signed, there was still no agreement on this.

[184:47]Nobody made any concessions on this.

[184:50]Iran never abandoned their intentions of controlling the strait.

[184:53]The United States never abandoned its goal of liberating the strait for freedom of navigation.

[184:58]So that was always going to be a problem.

[185:01]And by the way, Iran used the first violation to justify breaching the second.

[185:07]And by that, I mean Iran said, if there's no ceasefire in Lebanon, which they knew there

[185:12]wouldn't be, that is the pretext under which we will not implement the opening of the straight.

[185:18]They said, as long as the war in Lebanon is going on, we're not really going to open up

[185:22]the straight.

[185:23]And we're also not going to do the third thing, which is even begin negotiations on the nuclear

[185:28]program.

[185:29]And there's still no agreement on that either.

[185:35]Just like the original ceasefire, just like all these negotiations happening in Switzerland and Turkey and Islamabad and elsewhere,

[185:43]these negotiations are fundamentally not accomplishing anything.

[185:48]And I think what every single party recognizes, but what they are not telling us,

[185:53]what Israel and the Pentagon and Iran all assume, is that we are going back to war.

[186:01]It's not a question of if we are going back to war, the US, Israel, the Saudis, the Emiratis,

[186:07]we are all going back to war against Iran.

[186:11]And Iran knows that Iran anticipates this.

[186:13]They expect it.

[186:14]They're preparing for it.

[186:15]They know it's practically inevitable.

[186:18]It's just a question of when.

[186:20]And I think that both sides are simply just buying time.

[186:24]What we're really looking for is not peace.

[186:26]We're not actually solving anything.

[186:28]I don't know if there's much confidence

[186:30]that we will solve anything.

[186:31]What we're looking for is a reprieve from the pain.

[186:35]There's a midterm election coming up in November

[186:39]and gas prices have been too high,

[186:41]inflation has been ticking up.

[186:43]It's threatening to derail our AI driven

[186:46]economic growth broadly across the economy.

[186:49]So really, by any means necessary, we just need some oil getting out there.

[186:54]And I think that's fundamentally what the MOU was about.

[186:57]Did we really have confidence that Iran would open up the straight free and clear?

[187:01]Absolutely not.

[187:03]We knew their intention was to keep the straight closed under their management, but we knew

[187:08]that if we made a gesture, if we sweetened the deal by letting them get their oil out

[187:17]If we let the oil get out of the straight,

[187:20]if we let some of the other oil get out,

[187:22]even if they gotta pay,

[187:24]even if they need Iran's permission,

[187:27]even if some of the ships get attacked,

[187:29]hugging the coast of Oman,

[187:30]we said, hey, at least the markets

[187:33]are going to be optimistic.

[187:35]And so maybe we could stabilize the prices

[187:37]and keep them low.

[187:38]And this supply will increase a little bit

[187:42]on the global market.

[187:43]And I think that's really what this whole charade is about.

[187:46]Ever since the MOU was passed, this was about averting maybe the worst case scenario energy

[187:52]shortage ahead of the midterm elections.

[187:55]How do we just increase the global supply of energy a little bit?

[188:00]Let's let Iran sell their oil.

[188:02]How do we make it look like it's not a total capitulation?

[188:09]It would look like a capitulation if we let Iran sell their oil in dollars.

[188:15]When we are not allowed to get our oil out of there, and by that I mean our allied country's

[188:20]oil, the Saudis, Kuwait, the rest of them.

[188:23]So we did it in the framework of the MOU, you go first, you sell your oil, we won't

[188:29]sanction it and we'll even let you sell it in dollars.

[188:31]That's our gift to you.

[188:33]And also we knew it would be contentious in the straight and we knew it would be like

[188:37]this.

[188:38]We knew there'd be fighting.

[188:39]We knew there'd be bickering over it, but whether the tankers leave through the Iranian

[188:44]controlled route and they pay or need permission, whether they leave through the Omani route

[188:51]That's also increasing the supply of oil.

[188:54]So we can Huff and Puff and we could be upset about it and we could say they're in breach

[188:59]of the agreement and you know, all the rest of it, but the oil is hitting the market and

[189:04]maybe that's what matters in all of this.

[189:07]And if the oil is hitting the market, then we get an economic reprieve and you know what?

[189:11]We also get to buy time.

[189:17]and recover militarily,

[189:19]whether that's evacuating some of our soldiers,

[189:22]figuring out a different system of fortifications.

[189:25]We had a lot of our soldiers inside hotels.

[189:28]Hotels are not fortified against a drone strike.

[189:31]Iran knew where they were.

[189:33]The soldier stationed in those hotels knew Iran knew where they were,

[189:37]and they got hit, and those were the casualties.

[189:39]Those were the people that died.

[189:41]Six out of the 13 casualties were soldiers put up in a hotel. They knew they were being targeted. They knew they weren't safe and

[189:49]They weren't they got killed by a drone strike

[189:51]So it's buying us time and then on the other side the same thing is happening in Iran

[189:57]Iran has went back to work rebuilding their drone and missile production capability since April

[190:02]Ever since the heavy US bombardment stopped

[190:06]in the first week of April,

[190:07]I think it was April 7th or April 8th,

[190:10]beginning of the second week,

[190:11]Iran went right back to work

[190:13]rebuilding their missile and drone production capability,

[190:17]getting cheap supplies from China.

[190:20]And now the US intelligence for the military estimates

[190:24]that they still have 75% of their missile arsenal,

[190:27]they'll be back to 100% within six months.

[190:30]early as six months. So this is the United States buying time economically to

[190:36]avert a worst case scenario energy crunch. And this is, this is also buying

[190:42]time for Iran to get a little bit of cash, to pay their police and their

[190:45]military, getting a little bit of cash, and time to rebuild their drone and

[190:49]missile infrastructure as well. And I think what that represents is we're

[190:53]both getting ready for round two.

[190:55]We both think that we can fight the other two exhaustion.

[190:59]We both are still confident that we can get what we want,

[191:02]which is to say Iran is not,

[191:04]they're not waving the white flag.

[191:05]They still want control of the straight.

[191:07]They don't fear a return to hostilities.

[191:10]And on some level, neither do we.

[191:12]We're not willing to escalate necessarily

[191:15]by bombing their energy infrastructure.

[191:17]Maybe we'd be willing to do another assassination strike.

[191:21]But clearly we're not necessarily worried about the MOU falling apart either

[191:25]So I think like I said from the beginning this is not the end that to me

[191:31]That's always the story about Washington

[191:34]Tel Aviv and Tehran is that it just it's not over until it's over

[191:39]Last year after midnight hammer everybody said well, hey, no harm. No foul. We did one airstrike

[191:45]That's how it should be done. We got in we got out end of story

[191:50]And if there's anything to know about Iran is that it's never the end.

[191:56]We thought it was the end when Israel provoked Iran and Syria in April of 24.

[192:02]We thought it was the end in October 24, when Iran retaliated against Israel for their strike

[192:08]in Iran in July.

[192:10]We thought it was over in September 2025, when Israel did that assassination strike

[192:16]Now, we always think it's over in this whole thing, the ceasefire in Gaza, the ceasefire

[192:21]in Lebanon, the ceasefires with Iran, it's not over until it's over, until those fundamental

[192:28]root causes that are driving this escalation, driving the conflict are solved.

[192:33]And I think that what we effectively did with Midnight Hammer is we put ourselves on a pathway

[192:44]And by decisive, I mean, one side is gonna lose badly.

[192:50]Either Iran's regime will fall,

[192:52]or the United States will be dealt such a devastating blow,

[192:55]militarily or economically, that we have to leave.

[192:59]And maybe that means the regime

[193:00]with the Trump administration,

[193:02]maybe it goes on until 28,

[193:04]and a new president's elected

[193:05]on a promise of ending the war.

[193:07]I mean, but in some way, shape, or form,

[193:10]something's gotta give here,

[193:11]and it's gonna be with some finality.

[193:13]Because from a strategic point of view, we're not gonna let Iran have this nuclear stockpile,

[193:20]have a nuclear enrichment capability, have this native drone and missile capability,

[193:26]and we have no say, no check.

[193:29]No ability to enforce an agreement, no ability to trim the grass, intervene if it gets out

[193:34]of hand, especially not now, when the regime is absolutely defiant and dug in and resilient.

[193:42]And in many ways, vengeful and furious about what happened.

[193:46]So we can't let them alone and they can't leave us alone.

[193:50]They need to stick it to us so we don't try this again.

[193:53]So, like I said, I think when you consider what's happening on a deep level strategically,

[193:59]you realize it's not that the war isn't going to end, it's that the war cannot end unless

[194:05]it's decisive.

[194:06]So that's where we are.

[194:08]I don't, I don't see how this all comes together

[194:11]and gets wrapped up neatly and cleanly in a bow

[194:14]without one side coming down.

[194:16]That's where all this is headed.

[194:18]So that's the MOU.

[194:45]decision. Now, the news from today is that President Trump visited Ankara, the capital

[194:52]of Turkey for a NATO summit where he met with all the NATO leaders. And there were a lot

[197:04]country bought a Russian air defense system.

[197:28]He says, why wouldn't we give them F-35s or more loyal than other countries that should

[197:32]be loyal?

[197:33]Hmm.

[197:35]But whether Congress would try to block the sale remains unclear.

[197:39]At the end of Trump's first term, Congress passed a law to prevent Turkey from buying

[197:43]the planes as long as it has the Russian S-400 anti-aircraft system.

[197:48]Under the terms of the deal that was still being finalized during the gathering, Turkey

[197:52]would ship its S-400 system to a third country, helping to address the security concerns about

[197:58]F-35s being operated by the same militaries.

[198:03]A little bit of background on this, Turkey was actually part of the consortium going

[198:09]back to 1999, even to develop the F-35 jet. These jets are designed and developed and

[198:18]planned and built long before they actually are introduced into a country's arsenal.

[198:27]And in 1999, Turkey, along with other NATO countries, was part of that consortium to

[198:32]develop that next generation stealth fighter aircraft.

[198:37]And so, like other NATO countries, other U.S. allies, they were supposed to get them.

[198:43]However, in 2017, Turkey bought these S-400 air defense systems from Russia.

[198:50]And this is a time when Turkey was seeking a closer relationship with Russia.

[198:54]They were trying to play Russia and the United States off of each other,

[198:58]really in a bid to increase their leverage and their power as a middle power.

[199:10]that Turkey had an S-400 anti-aircraft system, what might have happened is that if Turkey

[199:33]And so its proprietary technology, what makes it so sophisticated, is the difficulty to

[199:38]detect this on radar.

[199:40]Well, the S400, an anti-aircraft system, the proprietary technology there is its radar.

[199:47]It's extremely sophisticated and advanced radar.

[199:56]could use the Russian anti-aircraft and its radar in particular to detect the F-35 and

[203:43]Now, the sale of F 35s to Turkey is largely symbolic. We're going to give them five fighter

[203:55]in order for Turkey to even have and operate the F-35s,

[204:12]for parts and maintenance and other things, what's more.

[204:36]And then the other problem is that this is going to have to go through Congress.

[204:47]And it also is going to have to go through Congress.

[205:07]So, is this meaningfully going to change anything?

[205:11]Not necessarily.

[205:13]But the problem that this poses to Israel is that the U.S. and Israel have always had

[205:18]this special relationship, as everybody knows.

[205:21]And the reason that Israel has a military advantage over its neighbors is because this

[205:26]is actually enshrined in federal law in the United States.

[205:30]There's a federal statute in America that says that when we sell military technology

[205:37]to countries in the Middle East, we can't do it in such a way that it gives those countries

[205:44]a military edge over Israel.

[205:50]And it is a legally binding mandate

[205:54]that whatever we give to Israel

[205:56]and whatever we give to any and every other country,

[205:59]we must ensure that Israel maintains

[206:01]the qualitative military edge over that country.

[206:05]So for example, we were giving F-35s,

[206:07]I think it was to the Emirates or Saudi Arabia recently,

[206:11]but we couldn't give them as many as we wanted to

[206:15]because we had to make sure that Israel

[206:17]always has more than that other country.

[206:20]because it is mandated that we got to protect Israel's military edge.

[206:24]It's not enough that Israel has a nuclear arsenal of 300 warheads.

[206:30]It's not enough that they have intelligence sharing and close coordination with the Pentagon.

[206:37]We also make sure that they have the best and most sophisticated equipment from a technological point of view,

[206:43]and that they have more of it.

[206:44]that they have more of it or enough of it that's efficient,

[206:47]that they will have the edge over the Emirates

[206:49]or Saudi Arabia or Qatar.

[206:52]And so the idea that we would give Turkey F 35s,

[206:56]what it represents is that we might try to balance things

[207:00]out in the Middle East.

[207:01]Maybe it's good for the United States to give Turkey

[207:05]relatively more power against Israel.

[207:08]We're turning up the heat. We're going to make it a little bit more uncomfortable. Let's say for the sake of argument

[207:13]there's a hypothetical conflict in Syria

[207:17]When the Assad regime was pushed out

[207:19]Erdogan had an idea to build a Turkish military base inside of Syria

[207:25]Well, Israel went ahead and bombed that base to ensure it couldn't be used by Turkey

[207:32]Already, there's going to be a struggle for power to fill that vacuum inside of Syria.

[207:38]And I think increasingly it's going to be militarized.

[207:41]Israel wants to maintain its freedom of operation in Syria, which is to say their ability to

[207:45]strike where and when they want with impunity.

[208:15]at the minimum to win an air war against Turkey in Syria.

[208:19]If Turkey has an inferior air force,

[208:21]then they might need to eventually bow out

[208:25]and withdraw from such a conflict.

[208:28]Well, if the United States is furnishing Turkey

[208:30]with the latest F-35 or some variant of F-35,

[208:34]then maybe that battle becomes a little bit closer.

[208:37]than it otherwise would be.
